title: Range of Motion (ROM) Exercises
subtitle: The nurse performs and teaches ROM exercises to prevent contractures — supporting the joint above and below, moving slowly to the point of resistance (never past pain), and encouraging active ROM when able.

patient.name: Range of Motion
patient.admitDate: The nurse performs and teaches range-of-motion exercises to maintain mobility and prevent contractures.

nursesNote: The nurse performs and teaches range-of-motion exercises to maintain joint mobility, preserve muscle tone, promote circulation, and prevent contractures. Active ROM, in which the patient moves independently, is encouraged whenever possible and can be built into bathing and dressing; active-assisted ROM is used when the patient has partial ability, and passive ROM is performed by the nurse for a patient who cannot move the joint. During the exercises the nurse supports the joint above and below, moves it slowly and smoothly through its full available range, and stops at the point of resistance — never forcing past pain. Each movement is repeated several times and done at least twice a day. The nurse does not force a contracted joint, stops if pain or spasm occurs, and reports any new or worsening limitation.
providerOrders: Perform range-of-motion exercises at least twice daily
Encourage active ROM when the patient is able
Support the joint above and below during passive ROM
Move each joint to the point of resistance, not past pain
Report any new pain, swelling, or loss of range

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about ROM exercises?

opts.text: ROM exercises help prevent contractures and maintain joint mobility
Passive ROM is done for a patient who cannot move the joint
The joint above and below should be supported during ROM
Joints should be moved past the point of pain to improve flexibility
Active ROM is encouraged when the patient is able
ROM also promotes circulation and maintains muscle tone
Movements should be slow and smooth, not forced
Passive ROM does not build strength the way active ROM does
ROM is stopped if it causes pain or spasm
Forcing a contracted joint quickly will restore its range
Active ROM can be built into bathing and dressing
Each joint is moved through its full available range

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order performing passive ROM on an arm

opts.text: Explain the procedure and position the patient comfortably
Support the joint above and below
Move the joint slowly and smoothly through its range
Stop at the point of resistance, not pain
Repeat each movement and reassess comfort
A patient who exercises the joints on their own
A comatose patient whose joints the nurse moves
A patient doing their own exercises during ADLs
A paralyzed limb moved gently by the nurse
A recovering patient exercising without help
A sedated patient on bed rest moved by staff
A patient walking and doing self-care
An unconscious patient whose nurse prevents contractures

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Resistance and pain occur during passive ROM
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop the movement at that point
Do not force the joint past resistance
Assess for pain, swelling, or stiffness
Document the limitation
Report a new or worsening limitation to the provider
Supporting the joint above and below during ROM
Moving a joint past the point of pain
Encouraging active ROM when the patient can
Forcing a contracted joint to straighten quickly
Moving slowly and smoothly through the range
Continuing ROM despite spasm and pain
Building ROM into bathing and dressing
Skipping ROM for an immobile patient to save time

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
